figures.gloss: The chronically ill New Hampshire Congregationalist (1821–1910) who, after treatment by Phineas Quimby (1862–65) and a healing while reading Matthew 9 following her fall on the ice in Lynn (February 1866), spent nine years producing Science and Health. She chartered the Church of Christ, Scientist (1879), ran the Massachusetts Metaphysical College (1881), rebuilt the church around the Boston Mother Church (1892–94), and founded The Christian Science Monitor at 87 (1908).
figures.facet: A woman founding and legally architecting a durable American denomination in the nineteenth century — and governing it posthumously: the Manual of The Mother Church still runs the institution by her fixed statutes, a dead founder ruling by law.
figures.provenance: Eddy, Science and Health with Key to the Scriptures (1875) and Retrospection and Introspection (1891); Gillian Gill, Mary Baker Eddy (1998).

figures.gloss: Eddy's textbook, first published in 1875 and revised until her death in 1910. In 1895 she ordained the Bible and Science and Health together as the church's 'dual and impersonal pastor': Sunday services worldwide consist of readings from both, with no sermon and no preacher.
figures.facet: A tradition that abolished preaching outright — doctrinal authority is fixed in a page rather than a pulpit, insulating the teaching from every future interpreter, including the church's own clergy-less readers.
figures.provenance: Science and Health (1875; final edition 1910); Manual of The Mother Church (1895).

figures.gloss: Eddy's definition of God through seven capitalized synonyms — Principle, Mind, Soul, Spirit, Life, Truth, Love. God is All-in-all; reality is entirely spiritual, Mind and its ideas; matter has no true existence. Because God is Principle, healing is held to be demonstrable with the exactness of a science.
figures.facet: New England Protestant theology pushed to its idealist limit: the personal sovereign of Puritanism becomes a demonstrable Principle, and prayer shifts from petition to proof.
figures.provenance: Science and Health, chap. 'Recapitulation' (p. 465); Stephen Gottschalk, The Emergence of Christian Science in American Religious Life (1973).

figures.gloss: The counterfeit of divine Mind: the false belief that life, substance, and intelligence reside in matter. Sin, disease, and death are its illusions, possessing no God-derived reality; its weaponized form, 'malicious animal magnetism' — hostile mental influence — preoccupied Eddy's later years.
figures.facet: Evil demoted from entity to error: the tradition's theodicy and its therapeutics are a single move — what has no reality need only be unmasked, not fought — which is why healing, not ethics or eschatology, is its central evidence.
figures.provenance: Science and Health (glossary, 'Mortal Mind'; chap. 'Animal Magnetism Unmasked'); Gillian Gill, Mary Baker Eddy (1998).

figures.gloss: Metaphysical healing practiced by Journal-listed practitioners trained through Primary and Normal class instruction: silent or audible argument affirming God's allness and the unreality of disease, undertaken in place of medicine. Reliance on treatment instead of medical care has drawn repeated legal battles, most sharply over the deaths of adherents' children.
figures.facet: The priest and the physician replaced by a single office: healing as the tradition's ongoing act of worship and its public test — every recovery is claimed as a demonstration, which also makes every failure a doctrinal event.
figures.provenance: Science and Health, chap. 'Christian Science Practice'; Rennie Schoepflin, Christian Science on Trial (2003).

figures.gloss: The Maine clockmaker and former stage mesmerist (1802–1866) who came to hold that disease is a false belief curable by correcting the sufferer's understanding rather than by drugs. Eddy was his patient from 1862 to 1865, dating her own recovery to his treatment before his death in January 1866 — weeks before her own fall on the ice in Lynn.
figures.facet: He is the corpus's headwater the tradition itself contests: Eddy came to deny any doctrinal debt to him, and the 1883–87 'Quimby controversy' — fought in print with his former patients and the Dressers — is the fight over whether Christian Science is a discovery or an inheritance.
figures.provenance: Quimby's surviving manuscripts (published posthumously, ed. Horatio Dresser, The Quimby Manuscripts, 1921); Gillian Gill, Mary Baker Eddy (1998); Robert Peel, Mary Baker Eddy: The Years of Discovery (1966), on the 1883–87 controversy.

figures.gloss: The church's midweek service: readings from the Bible and Science and Health followed by spontaneous, unscripted testimonies from the congregation recounting healings and answered prayer. Eddy instituted the form, and it remains the standing public venue where the claim of a 'demonstrable Science' is performed case by case.
figures.facet: It converts the tradition's central truth-claim into a recurring, congregation-level burden of proof — unlike a creed recited once, the testimony meeting requires the church to keep producing fresh evidence, indefinitely, in public.
figures.provenance: Manual of The Mother Church (Eddy, various editions from 1895); Rennie Schoepflin, Christian Science on Trial (2003), on the testimony meeting's evidentiary function.

figures.gloss: The Boston church — original edifice consecrated 1894, domed extension added 1906 — of which every branch congregation worldwide is legally a satellite, chartered and governed under the Manual of The Mother Church that Eddy wrote and fixed before her death.
figures.facet: It is the axis-mundi built as a legal instrument: Eddy's authority did not end with her death in 1910 because she encoded it into the Manual's bylaws, so the Mother Church still governs every branch by a dead founder's standing statute rather than by any living successor's discretion.
figures.provenance: Manual of The Mother Church (Eddy, final edition 1910, still in force); Rolf Swensen's architectural-institutional histories of the Original and Extension edifices (1894, 1906).

figures.gloss: The statutory religious exemptions from child-neglect and manslaughter laws that most US states enacted for prayer-only healing from the 1970s onward (tied to a 1974 federal child-abuse-prevention regulation), and the criminal prosecutions of Christian Science parents that nonetheless followed when children died of medically treatable conditions under Christian Science treatment alone — most prominently Commonwealth v. Twitchell (Massachusetts, 1990–93).
figures.facet: The tradition's central proof-practice — healing demonstrated instead of medicine administered — collided with the state's child-protection power exactly where it could not be walked back: a dead child is not a contested testimony.
figures.provenance: Commonwealth v. Twitchell, 416 Mass. 114 (1993); Caroline Fraser, God's Perfect Child: Christian Science and the Church of Christ, Scientist (1999); Rita Swan / CHILD, Inc. advocacy and case records.

edges.note: The book is Eddy's 'discovery' fixed in final form — she revised it for thirty-five years and then bound her church to it by statute.
Divine Mind is the sole reality; mortal mind is its illusory negative — the whole system is this single inversion worked out.
Treatment exists to dissolve mortal mind's claims — the rite is the doctrine of error applied case by case.

GOLD SEAM: Shared Quimby milieu — Eddy was Quimby's patient (1862–65) and the Dressers carried his mind-cure into New Thought; but a seam, not a subset: Eddy denied the debt, the 1883–87 'Quimby controversy' litigated it, and the direction of dependence remains contested.

GOLD SEAM: Emma Curtis Hopkins, Eddy's student and Journal editor (1884–85), left to become New Thought's 'teacher of teachers' — Christian Science vocabulary flowed into New Thought through her classroom even as Eddy anathematized mind-cure.
GOLD SEAM: Eddy's Congregationalist inheritance radicalized — the Protestant relocation of authority from clergy to text is completed by ordaining two books as the church's only pastor.
Patient and healer, 1862–65 — the relationship whose interpretation (discovery vs. inheritance) is contested by the tradition itself.
Quimby's diagnosis of disease as false belief is the direct doctrinal seed Eddy's 'mortal mind' elaborates into a full theology of error.
Quimby's silent mental treatment of patients is the documented precursor practice to Christian Science treatment's method, though not its later evidentiary or ecclesial framing.

Quimby is the acknowledged common headwater of both traditions' mind-cure — his patients and students (via the Dressers and Warren Felt Evans) carried his method into New Thought independently of Eddy's church.
The testimony meeting is where the results of treatment are publicly reported and become the tradition's standing evidence.
The meeting's liturgical form begins with readings from the 'dual and impersonal pastor' before the floor opens to testimony.
Eddy founded, financed, and legally fixed the church's governing Manual before her death — the institution is her posthumous instrument.
The prosecutions and exemption statutes are direct legal consequences of relying on treatment instead of medical care for children.

Two unrelated traditions whose doctrinal restriction on standard medical intervention has produced real, documented court cases over the medical treatment of minors — a structural convergence, not a shared history.
Hopkins was Eddy's student and, briefly, her Christian Science Journal editor (1884–85) before the break that sent her into New Thought.

dossier.essence: Christian Science is the metaphysical-healing church founded by Mary Baker Eddy, who dated her 'discovery' to a healing at Lynn, Massachusetts, in February 1866, published Science and Health in 1875, and chartered the Church of Christ, Scientist, in 1879, rebuilding it around the Boston Mother Church. Its central claim is that reality is wholly spiritual and disease an illusion of 'mortal mind,' dispellable by prayer with — the tradition insists — the exactness of a science; from a US peak near 269,000 members counted in 1936 it has undergone a long, documented decline.
dossier.cosmos: God is defined through seven synonyms — Principle, Mind, Soul, Spirit, Life, Truth, Love — and is All-in-all: reality consists of divine Mind and its ideas, and matter has no true existence. Sin, disease, and death are errors of 'mortal mind,' the counterfeit sense of life in matter; death itself is an illusion, existence continuing after 'passing on' until every error is outgrown, with heaven and hell understood as present states of thought rather than places.
dossier.practice: There is no clergy: elected lay Readers conduct Sunday services consisting of readings from the Bible and Science and Health, ordained together in 1895 as the church's 'dual and impersonal pastor,' with Wednesday meetings for testimonies of healing. Journal-listed practitioners give metaphysical treatment in place of medicine — a substitution that has drawn repeated litigation, most sharply over the deaths of members' children — and the Manual of The Mother Church still governs the institution by Eddy's fixed statutes.
dossier.canon: The Bible together with Mary Baker Eddy's Science and Health with Key to the Scriptures (1875; final revision 1910), the church's joint 'pastor,' governed institutionally by her Manual of The Mother Church (1895).
dossier.reflects: The therapeutic facet of the religious impulse made the entire system — healing as worship, evidence, and argument at once, religion recast as demonstrable science in the idiom of the nineteenth century.
sources: Stephen Gottschalk, 'The Emergence of Christian Science in American Religious Life' (1973)
Rennie B. Schoepflin, 'Christian Science on Trial: Religious Healing in America' (2003)
Mary Baker Eddy, 'Science and Health with Key to the Scriptures' (1875; final revision 1910)
